question_text: Decentralisation means:
explanation: Decentralisation refers to the systematic delegation of authority at every level of management and in all departments. It is an extension of delegation — when delegation becomes a policy applied throughout the organisation, it is called decentralisation. Decision-making authority is distributed to lower levels of the hierarchy.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Concentrating all decision-making power with top management
Delegating authority to only one subordinate
Systematic delegation of authority at every level and in all departments throughout the organisation
Removing all levels of management

elimination_hints.hint: Concentrating power at the top is centralisation — the exact opposite of decentralisation.
Delegation to one subordinate is a single act of delegation, not decentralisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a single delegation with organisation-wide policy

elimination_hints.hint: Decentralisation distributes authority across levels — it does not remove them.

question_text: Centralisation refers to:
explanation: Centralisation means that all decision-making authority is concentrated at the top level of management. In a centralised organisation, important decisions are made by top management and lower-level managers have little or no authority to make decisions on their own.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Concentration of decision-making authority at the top level
Distribution of authority to all levels of management
Giving complete freedom to all employees
Having only one department in the organisation

elimination_hints.hint: Distribution of authority to all levels describes decentralisation, not centralisation.
Complete freedom to employees is more aligned with decentralisation, not centralisation.
Centralisation is about decision-making authority, not the number of departments.

question_text: Which of the following is an importance of decentralisation?
explanation: Decentralisation develops initiative among subordinates because they get the authority to make decisions on their own. When managers at lower levels can take independent decisions, they develop confidence, problem-solving skills, and a sense of ownership. This creates a pool of competent managers for future leadership roles.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: It keeps all power with one person
It develops initiative and promotes a sense of responsibility among subordinates
It makes decision-making slower
It increases bureaucratic delays

elimination_hints.hint: Keeping all power with one person is centralisation — decentralisation distributes it.
Decentralisation speeds up decision-making by allowing decisions at lower levels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the effect on decision speed

elimination_hints.hint: Decentralisation reduces bureaucratic delays by cutting down the need for approval from top.

question_text: Decentralisation relieves the top management from:
explanation: Decentralisation relieves top management from the burden of making routine decisions. When lower-level managers handle day-to-day operational decisions, top management is free to concentrate on strategic planning, policy formulation, and overall coordination of the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: All responsibilities and accountability
The need to exist as a management level
Strategic planning and policy-making
The burden of routine operational decisions, freeing them for strategic matters

elimination_hints.hint: Top management retains overall accountability — decentralisation does not remove it.
Top management always exists — decentralisation empowers other levels, not eliminates the top.
Strategic planning is what top management focuses ON after being freed from routine decisions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing what top management does vs. is freed from

question_text: Decentralisation facilitates 'growth and diversification' because:
explanation: Decentralisation facilitates growth because each product or region can be managed by a separate division or department with its own decision-making authority. This allows the organisation to enter new markets, launch new products, and expand geographically without overwhelming top management.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Each product or region can have independent decision-making, allowing expansion without overloading top management
The company stops making any new products
Top management handles every decision for every product
The organisation reduces its size to become more manageable

elimination_hints.hint: Growth means expanding products and markets — not stopping production.
If top management handled every decision, growth would be impossible due to overload — decentralisation prevents this.
Decentralisation enables growth — it does not require shrinking.

question_text: Decentralisation promotes 'better control' through:
explanation: Decentralisation enables better control because each department or division can set its own performance standards and evaluate results at the local level. This is faster and more accurate than waiting for top management to review everything. Performance can be measured division-wise, making it easier to identify problem areas.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Having one person check everything in the organisation
Removing all standards and benchmarks
Each department evaluating its own performance against local standards, enabling quicker corrective action
Ignoring performance data from lower levels

elimination_hints.hint: One person checking everything is centralised control — it is slow and ineffective for large organisations.
Standards are essential for control — removing them would make control impossible.
Performance data from all levels is valuable — ignoring it defeats the purpose of control.

question_text: What is the key difference between delegation and decentralisation?
explanation: Delegation is a one-to-one relationship between a superior and a subordinate where authority for a specific task is transferred. Decentralisation is the systematic delegation of authority throughout the organisation at all levels and departments. Delegation is a management practice; decentralisation is an organisational philosophy or policy.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Delegation involves lower levels while decentralisation involves only top management
Delegation is permanent while decentralisation is temporary
They are exactly the same concept
Delegation is between a superior and subordinate for specific tasks; decentralisation is systematic delegation throughout the organisation as a policy

elimination_hints.hint: Both involve lower levels — the difference is scope, not which level is involved.
Delegation can be temporary or ongoing; decentralisation as an organisational policy is typically enduring.
They are related but distinct — delegation is a process; decentralisation is the policy of applying it organisation-wide.

question_text: In which of the following situations would decentralisation be more appropriate?
explanation: Decentralisation is more appropriate when the organisation is large, has diverse products, and operates in different geographical locations. In such cases, decisions need to be made quickly at local levels by people who understand the local conditions, customer preferences, and market dynamics.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: A small family-run business with 5 employees
A large corporation with diverse products operating in multiple regions
A startup with one product and one office
A sole proprietorship with no employees

elimination_hints.hint: A small business with 5 employees can be easily managed centrally — decentralisation is unnecessary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying decentralisation to very small firms

elimination_hints.hint: A startup with one product and one office can be efficiently run with centralised decisions.
A sole proprietorship has no subordinates — there is no one to decentralise to.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying decentralisation where delegation is impossible

question_text: Tata Group allows each company (TCS, Tata Motors, Tata Steel) to operate with significant autonomy in decision-making. This is an example of:
explanation: Tata Group's approach of giving significant decision-making autonomy to each of its companies is a classic example of decentralisation. Each company (TCS, Tata Motors, Tata Steel) operates as a separate entity with its own management making decisions suited to its industry, while the parent group provides overall strategic direction.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Decentralisation
Centralisation
Functional structure only
Informal organisation

elimination_hints.hint: Centralisation would mean Tata Group's top management making all decisions for every company — the opposite of what is described.
Functional structure is about grouping by function — the scenario describes decision-making autonomy.
This is a formal, planned arrangement — not spontaneous informal relationships.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing formal decentralisation with informal organisation

question_text: Which of the following is a difference between delegation and decentralisation?
explanation: In delegation, a superior delegates authority to a specific subordinate — the superior retains control and can take back the authority anytime. In decentralisation, authority is distributed throughout the organisation as a matter of policy and it is not easily withdrawn. Delegation is narrow in scope; decentralisation is wide.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Delegation is always permanent; decentralisation is always temporary
Delegation applies to the whole organisation; decentralisation applies to one person
Delegation is narrow in scope (superior to subordinate); decentralisation is wide (throughout the organisation)
There is no practical difference between the two

elimination_hints.hint: Delegation can be temporary or ongoing; decentralisation is generally a long-term policy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the permanence of each concept

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the scope — delegation is narrow (one person), decentralisation is organisation-wide.
They are related but have clear differences in scope, purpose, and application.

question_text: A retail chain allows its store managers in different cities to decide on local promotions, product displays, and staffing without seeking head office approval. This practice demonstrates:
explanation: When store managers across the organisation are empowered to make local decisions independently, it shows systematic delegation throughout the organisation — which is decentralisation. Each manager has autonomy to respond to local market conditions, customer preferences, and operational needs.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Centralisation of authority
Decentralisation of authority
Informal organisation
Scalar chain violation

elimination_hints.hint: In centralisation, store managers would need head office approval — the opposite of what is described.
This is a deliberate management policy, not spontaneous social interaction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing formal policy with informal organisation

elimination_hints.hint: The scalar chain is about formal communication — this scenario is about decision-making authority.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up communication and authority concepts

question_text: In a large hospital, the head doctor delegates surgical scheduling to the chief surgeon, ward management to the head nurse, and procurement to the admin officer. Each reports back to the head doctor. Which element of delegation is illustrated by 'reporting back'?
explanation: Reporting back to the head doctor illustrates accountability — the obligation to report on the outcome of the delegated task. Each person is answerable to the head doctor for their performance. While authority was delegated, the head doctor requires accountability through these reports.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Authority
Responsibility
Accountability
Departmentalisation

elimination_hints.hint: Authority is the right to give orders — reporting back is about answering for results.
Responsibility is the obligation to perform the task — reporting back is about being answerable for outcomes.
Departmentalisation is about grouping activities — not about reporting relationships.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a feature of formal organisation?
explanation: Formal organisation is characterised by defined structure, official rules, specified roles, and clear authority relationships. Spontaneous social interactions are a feature of informal organisation, not formal. Formal organisation is planned and deliberate, while social interactions arise naturally.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: It is deliberately created by management
It has clear rules and procedures
It specifies authority-responsibility relationships
It is based on spontaneous social interactions

elimination_hints.hint: Being deliberately created is a defining feature of formal organisation.
Clear rules and procedures are central to formal organisation.
Specifying authority-responsibility relationships is a core feature of formal organisation.

question_text: In an IT company, developers from different teams form a cricket group that meets every weekend. During breaks, they also share technical tips and help each other debug code. This is an example of:
explanation: This is an example of informal organisation. The cricket group and the knowledge-sharing network have emerged spontaneously based on common interests (cricket) and mutual help (debugging), not through any formal management directive. These personal relationships coexist alongside the formal organisational structure.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Formal organisation
Informal organisation
Divisional structure
Decentralisation

elimination_hints.hint: The cricket group was not created by management — it formed spontaneously, so it is informal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating any workplace group as formal

elimination_hints.hint: Divisional structure is a formal way of organising — not a social group.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing social groups with formal structure

elimination_hints.hint: Decentralisation is about authority distribution — not about social groups forming.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up authority concepts with social dynamics

question_text: A company with divisions in 5 states allows each state head to decide pricing, advertising, and local partnerships independently. The headquarters only sets the overall brand guidelines. This represents:
explanation: This is decentralisation at work. The company has systematically granted decision-making authority to state-level heads for key functions (pricing, advertising, partnerships), while retaining only strategic brand decisions at the centre. This allows each state to respond to local market conditions while maintaining brand consistency.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Complete centralisation
Informal organisation
Functional structure
Decentralisation with centralised brand strategy

elimination_hints.hint: In complete centralisation, state heads would not have any decision-making authority — the opposite of what is described.
This is a formal policy of authority distribution, not spontaneous social interaction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing formal policy with informal organisation

elimination_hints.hint: Functional structure groups by function — this scenario is about geographical authority distribution.

question_text: Departmentalisation refers to:
explanation: Departmentalisation is the process of grouping similar activities into departments, divisions, or sections. After work has been divided into individual tasks, these tasks are grouped together based on some common basis (function, product, territory, etc.) to form departments. This is the second step in the organising process.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Grouping similar activities into departments or divisions
Hiring employees for each department
Closing down unprofitable departments
Merging all departments into one

elimination_hints.hint: Hiring employees is part of staffing, not departmentalisation.
Closing departments is a strategic decision, not what departmentalisation means.
Merging all into one is the opposite of creating departments — departmentalisation creates separate groups.

question_text: Which of the following correctly states the relationship between delegation and decentralisation?
explanation: Decentralisation is the end result of systematic delegation. When delegation is done as a policy at every level and across all departments, the outcome is decentralisation. Delegation is the process; decentralisation is the result when this process is applied comprehensively throughout the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: They are completely unrelated concepts
Decentralisation is the end result of systematic and widespread delegation throughout the organisation
Delegation leads to centralisation
Decentralisation must happen before delegation can occur

elimination_hints.hint: They are closely related — decentralisation is built upon delegation.
Delegation distributes authority downward — this leads to decentralisation, not centralisation.
Delegation is the building block — it happens first, and systematic delegation leads to decentralisation.

question_text: An organisation chart showing positions from Managing Director at the top, then General Managers, then Departmental Managers, then Supervisors, and then Workers at the bottom represents:
explanation: This describes the formal organisation structure with its hierarchy clearly depicted in an organisation chart. The chart shows the official chain of command from the highest level (Managing Director) to the lowest level (Workers), with each level having defined authority and reporting relationships.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Formal organisation with clear hierarchy and chain of command
Informal organisation based on personal relationships
Decentralised decision-making
A matrix organisation structure

elimination_hints.hint: Informal organisation cannot be depicted on an official chart — it is unplanned and undocumented.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking informal organisation has a chart

elimination_hints.hint: The chart shows hierarchy, not necessarily decentralised decision-making — hierarchy can exist in both centralised and decentralised settings.
Matrix structure has dual reporting (to functional and project heads) — this chart shows a simple single-line hierarchy.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT an importance of organising?
explanation: Organising leads to benefits of specialisation, clarity in working relationships, optimum utilisation of resources, adaptation to change, effective administration, development of personnel, and expansion and growth. However, organising does NOT guarantee that all employees will be motivated. Motivation depends on directing, not organising.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Benefits of specialisation
Clarity in working relationships
Guaranteed motivation of all employees
Optimum utilisation of resources

elimination_hints.hint: Specialisation is a well-established importance of organising.
Clarity in working relationships is a key importance of organising.
Optimum resource utilisation is a recognised importance of organising.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising resource efficiency as a benefit

question_text:
Assertion (A): Decentralisation is an extension of delegation.
Reason (R): When delegation is done systematically at all levels of an organisation, it results in decentralisation.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Delegation is the process of granting authority from superior to subordinate. When this process is applied as a policy throughout the organisation at every level, the outcome is decentralisation. Thus, decentralisation is indeed an extension of delegation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains why decentralisation is an extension of delegation — it is the correct explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the causal link between A and R

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is also true — systematic delegation at all levels does result in decentralisation.
The assertion is true — decentralisation is widely recognised as an extension of delegation.
